question: What is a Riemannian metric and what does it let you do?

options: A smoothly-varying positive-definite inner product $g_p$ on each tangent space; it defines lengths of curves, angles, volumes, the Levi-Civita connection, geodesics, and curvature — turning a smooth manifold into a metric-measure geometric object
A metric is just a distance function with no tensor structure (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception)
It is an antisymmetric 2-form (that is a symplectic form) (not supported by the evidence) (this reverses the actual relationship)
A Riemannian metric must be flat everywhere (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)
passive.explanation: A Riemannian metric $g = g_{ij}dx^i dx^j$ assigns an inner product to each tangent space, varying smoothly and symmetric positive-definite. Arc length: $L(\gamma) = \int\sqrt{g(\dot\gamma,\dot\gamma)}\,dt$; the Riemannian distance is the infimum over paths. The metric uniquely determines the Levi-Civita connection (torsion-free, metric-compatible), whence geodesics and curvature. Volume form $\sqrt{\det g}\,d^nx$. A Lorentzian metric (signature $-+++$) instead underlies general relativity. Riemannian geometry is the study of these intrinsic geometric structures and how curvature controls global shape.

active.prompt: Match each curvature/connection object with its meaning:

pairs.definition: Unique torsion-free, metric-compatible connection; Christoffel symbols $\Gamma^k_{ij}$ from $g$
$R^l_{ijk}$; measures path-dependence of parallel transport; vanishes iff locally flat
Contractions of Riemann; control volume growth; source side of Einstein's equations
Gauss curvature of 2-planes in $T_pM$; positive (sphere), zero (flat), negative (hyperbolic)

active.explanation: Curvature is the local obstruction to flatness. Parallel-transporting a vector around a small loop rotates it by an amount set by the Riemann tensor. Constant-curvature model spaces — sphere ($K>0$), Euclidean ($K=0$), hyperbolic ($K<0$) — anchor comparison geometry, where curvature bounds control distances, volumes, and topology.

question: What is parallel transport and how does it reveal curvature?

options: Transporting a vector along a curve keeping it 'as constant as possible' ($\nabla_{\dot\gamma}V = 0$); around a closed loop the vector returns rotated by an amount determined by the Riemann curvature enclosed — holonomy. Flat space gives zero rotation, path-independence
Parallel transport always returns a vector unchanged (this describes a different phenomenon) (a common misconception) (this conflates cause and effect)
It depends only on endpoints, never the path (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence)
Curvature has no effect on transported vectors (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: A connection $\nabla$ specifies how to differentiate vector fields and hence how to parallel-transport: solve $\dot V^k + \Gamma^k_{ij}\dot\gamma^i V^j = 0$ along $\gamma$. On a curved manifold transport is path-dependent; the net rotation around an infinitesimal loop is $R(X,Y)V$ — this is the operational definition of the Riemann tensor. The set of holonomy rotations forms the holonomy group (Berger's classification constrains which groups arise — e.g. special holonomy $G_2$, $\text{SU}(n)$ Calabi-Yau, $\text{Sp}(n)$). Classic illustration: transporting a vector around a spherical triangle returns it rotated by the enclosed solid angle (= area $\times K$), the Gauss-Bonnet seed.

question: What is a geodesic, and in what sense does it generalise a straight line?

options: A curve with zero covariant acceleration $\nabla_{\dot\gamma}\dot\gamma = 0$: locally length-minimising and 'as straight as possible'; in coordinates $\ddot x^k + \Gamma^k_{ij}\dot x^i\dot x^j = 0$. Free particles in GR follow geodesics of spacetime
A geodesic is any curve at all (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Geodesics always globally minimise length (not supported by the evidence) (this conflates cause and effect) (this reverses the actual relationship)
A geodesic must be a closed loop (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Geodesics are the manifold's straight lines: their tangent is parallel-transported along themselves (zero geodesic curvature). They extremise arc length (locally minimise, though not always globally — e.g. great circles on a sphere overshoot past the antipode). The geodesic equation is a second-order ODE, so a geodesic is determined by a point and an initial velocity (the exponential map $\exp_p$). Conjugate points (where nearby geodesics refocus) signal the breakdown of minimisation and are controlled by curvature (Jacobi equation). In physics, the geodesic principle is how gravity becomes geometry: matter falls along spacetime geodesics.

question: State the Gauss-Bonnet theorem and its significance.

options: For a compact oriented surface, $\int_M K\,dA = 2\pi\chi(M)$: the total Gaussian curvature equals $2\pi$ times the Euler characteristic — a local geometric quantity integrates to a topological invariant, independent of how the surface is bent
Total curvature is always zero (true only as a special case, not in general) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
It relates curvature to the surface area only (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
It holds only for flat tori (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship)
passive.explanation: Gauss-Bonnet is the archetype of a local-to-global theorem: pointwise curvature $K$ integrates to $2\pi\chi$, where $\chi = 2-2g$ for a genus-$g$ surface. So a sphere ($\chi=2$) must have total curvature $4\pi$ no matter how you deform it; a torus ($\chi=0$) has total curvature zero. The boundary version adds geodesic curvature and exterior angle terms (explaining the spherical-triangle angle excess). It is the 2D case of the Chern-Gauss-Bonnet theorem (Euler class in all even dimensions) and the Atiyah-Singer index theorem. It cleanly shows geometry constrains topology and vice versa.

question: How do curvature bounds control the global structure of a manifold?

options: Comparison theorems: e.g. Bonnet-Myers (positive Ricci lower bound $\Rightarrow$ compact, finite fundamental group, bounded diameter); Cartan-Hadamard (non-positive curvature, complete, simply connected $\Rightarrow$ diffeomorphic to $\mathbb R^n$ via exp); Toponogov/Rauch compare triangles and geodesic spreading to model spaces
Curvature bounds say nothing about global topology (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon)
Positive curvature forces a manifold to be non-compact (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)
All curvature bounds give the same conclusion (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
passive.explanation: A central theme: pinching curvature constrains size, topology, and shape. Bonnet-Myers: if $\mathrm{Ric} \ge (n-1)k > 0$ then diameter $\le \pi/\sqrt k$, so $M$ is compact with finite $\pi_1$ (rules out, e.g., a positively-Ricci-curved infinite cylinder). Cartan-Hadamard: complete simply-connected with $K\le 0$ is diffeomorphic to $\mathbb R^n$ (no conjugate points; exp is a covering) — hyperbolic-type rigidity. Rauch/Toponogov comparison underlie the sphere theorem (1/4-pinched $\Rightarrow$ homeomorphic to a sphere), Cheeger-Gromov compactness, and the proof of the differentiable sphere theorem via Ricci flow. Curvature is the master control on Riemannian geometry's global behaviour.

question: What is the Atiyah-Singer index theorem, in spirit?

options: For an elliptic differential operator $D$ on a compact manifold, the analytic index (dim ker $-$ dim coker) equals a topological index computed from characteristic classes (curvature integrals); it unifies Gauss-Bonnet, Riemann-Roch, and Hirzebruch signature theorems
It states that all operators have zero index (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
It is a purely algebraic statement with no geometry (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect)
It applies only to first-order ODEs (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Atiyah-Singer (1963) is one of the great unifying theorems of 20th-century mathematics. The analytic index of an elliptic operator (a solution-counting quantity, e.g. zero modes of the Dirac operator) is invariant under deformation and equals an integral of characteristic classes over the manifold (the topological index). Special cases: Chern-Gauss-Bonnet (Euler operator $\to \chi$), Hirzebruch-Riemann-Roch (Dolbeault operator on complex manifolds), the signature theorem, and the spin Dirac index ($\hat A$-genus). It is indispensable in theoretical physics: anomalies in gauge/gravitational theories are index densities (Adler-Bell-Jackiw), instanton zero modes, and the counting of fermionic ground states all follow from it. Geometry, analysis, and topology meet.
